questions.text: I find that my best days are the ones where I spent most of my time helping others.
I review my commitments at the start of each week to ensure nothing is forgotten.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the ethical truth that should temper every exercise of authority and every allocation of advantage.
I believe that cultivating my own inner life—through reflection, learning, and growth—is my highest duty.
I am the one who usually initiates the post-mortem and names what went wrong.
I tend to miss when someone withdraws from a conversation as a silent protest.
I experience emotional pain as a dull awareness that fades predictably over time.
I find that I rarely feel the urge to physically discharge emotional energy through movement or pacing.
I notice that transitions between emotional states happen gradually for me.
I notice when someone carefully chooses words to avoid offending a specific person in the room.
I would rather be exhausted from helping than rested from self-care.
I think that the ability to hold suffering at an analytical distance is what makes it possible to respond to it systematically rather than haphazardly.
I am the type of person who builds the roadmap.
I jump between tasks based on energy and interest rather than following a logical sequence.
I believe that the ability to maintain a cool, logical perspective when confronted with suffering is a form of competence that the world needs more of.
I believe that the moral significance of suffering is what gives ethical reasoning its urgency and its necessity.
I feel comfortable in ambiguous situations where no one has taken charge yet.
I hold the view that patience in execution is what turns a good idea into a finished product.
I believe that calm, measured analysis of a painful situation is more likely to lead to an effective and fair response than emotional involvement.
I find that I adapt to the tone already present in groups I join.
I tend to make sure my own needs are fully met before turning my attention outward.
I do not naturally notice when someone is subtly coaching another person through a social situation.
I experience the first moments of waking up after a bad dream as indistinguishable from the terror of the dream itself.
I find that preparation reduces my anxiety and increases my confidence.
I find that I am generally unaware of when people around me are managing each other's emotions.
I routinely structure my life to maximize personal freedom and opportunity.
I feel like I can sense what a group's unwritten rules are without being told.
I hold the view that generosity with my time is the highest form of generosity.
My emotional reactions are quick to fire and slow to fade.
I hold the view that the moral demand of suffering is not merely a feeling but a genuine perception of ethical reality.
I typically introduce structure to group work before anyone else does.
I value adaptability more than predictability in how I approach my day.
I welcome unexpected changes because they often lead somewhere more interesting.
I am the one who asks the group to co-create success criteria.
I notice micro-expressions that flash across someone's face during conversation.
I notice that my body reacts to emotional memories as if they're happening in real time.
